turns.user_message: Honestly, I'm really struggling. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit three times before. The longest I lasted was three months, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself.
step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Honestly, I'm really struggling. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit three times before. The longest I lasted was three months, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself.
Therapist: I can sense your frustration. It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge that and to be willing to try again. What makes you want to quit this time?

Patient: My daughter. She's getting older, and I don't want her to see me smoking. I'm worried about the example I'm setting for her. And to be honest, I'm also worried about my health. I've been experiencing some breathing issues lately, and I know it's related to smoking.

Therapist: That's a great motivator. It sounds like you're not just doing this for yourself, but for your daughter as well. What kind of support system do you have in place to help you quit this time?

Patient: Well, that's the thing. I don't really have one. My husband is a social smoker, and my friends all smoke. They don't understand why I can't just cut down instead of quitting altogether. And my work schedule is so busy, I don't have time for support groups or counseling.
Current patient message:

Well, that's the thing. I don't really have one. My husband is a social smoker, and my friends all smoke. They don't understand why I can't just cut down instead of quitting altogether. And my work schedule is so busy, I don't have time for support groups or counseling.
Therapist:
step7_response: It sounds like having people around you who smoke can make it harder for you to quit. How do you think you would feel if you were surrounded by people who didn't smoke, or if your husband didn't smoke at all?

turns.user_message: Yeah, I've been using some online forums and apps, but it's hard to connect with others. It feels like I'm going through this alone. And sometimes the advice they give just doesn't resonate with me.
step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
